title: Making a Family App Feel Like an Apple Product: Design Tokens + AI Implementation
summary: Once the functionality works, interfaces often feel like tools rather than products. After implementing Apple's design token system, dark mode, and glassmorphism, it started feeling like a real product. This post covers how I used the /apple-style-ui skill to drive AI through this visual upgrade, including the WCAG contrast traps we hit along the way.
body:
## When Function Works But Feel Doesn't

The family calendar functionality was running on day one. But opening the page felt like: this is a tool, not a product.

UIs written with Tailwind have a specific character—clean, structured, but also flat. For an app that family members will use daily, that's not enough. What I wanted was: opening it should spark a tiny bit of delight, not "oh right, this is that calendar my husband/wife made."

So I added a task: redesign the visual layer using the `/apple-style-ui` skill.

---

## What's at the Core of Apple's Design Language

Before letting AI loose, I went through the design principles in the apple-style-ui skill. A few core concepts worth noting:

**Clarity**: Content is the hero, UI chrome should disappear. Translucent surfaces, thin borders, restrained color use—if a UI element doesn't serve content, delete it.

**Depth**: Layered surfaces create spatial hierarchy. Each layer has its purpose:

| Layer | Purpose | Blur | Opacity |
|-------|---------|------|--------|
| 0 | Wallpaper/background | — | — |
| 1 | Main container | blur(40px) | High (0.72) |
| 2 | Cards | blur(20px) | Medium (0.45-0.65) |
| 3 | Popover/menus | blur(40px) | High (0.72) |
| 4 | Content area | None | solid |

**Key rule**: blur for chrome, solid for content. Anything users need to read (text, data) must have solid backgrounds, or readability suffers.

---

## Implementing the Token System

AI established a complete CSS variable system in `src/app/globals.css`:

```css
:root {
  /* System accent colors */
  --apple-blue:   #007aff;
  --apple-green:  #34c759;
  --apple-red:    #ff3b30;
  
  /* Label hierarchy */
  --apple-label:   #1d1d1f;
  --apple-label-2: rgba(60,60,67,0.72);
  --apple-label-3: #6e6e75;
  
  /* Spacing (4pt grid) */
  --s1:4px; --s2:8px; --s3:12px; --s4:16px;
  
  /* Border radius */
  --r-xs:6px; --r-sm:8px; --r-md:12px;
  --r-lg:16px; --r-xl:18px; --r-2xl:20px;
}

.dark {
  --apple-label:   rgba(255,255,255,0.92);
  --apple-label-2: rgba(255,255,255,0.70);
  --apple-label-3: rgba(255,255,255,0.55);
}
```

A few design decisions in this system worth noting:

**Label hierarchy**: Three-tier label colors correspond to different information weights. Primary titles use `--apple-label`, secondary info uses `--apple-label-2`, supporting text uses `--apple-label-3`. In light mode they have different opacities, in dark mode they invert to white variants.

**4pt spacing grid**: All spacing is multiples of 4. `--s1=4px, --s2=8px, --s3=12px...`. This isn't a rule, it's a constraint—preventing AI from randomly using 5px, 7px and other rhythm-breaking numbers.

**Glassmorphism components**: UpcomingChips, AgentCard, FabBar all use `backdrop-blur` + translucent background combinations:

```css
bg-white/88 dark:bg-white/[0.06]
border border-white/90 dark:border-white/[0.07]
backdrop-blur-xl
```

---

## Dark Mode: An Overlooked Trap

The implementation choice for dark mode matters. There are two approaches:

1. **`prefers-color-scheme` media query**: Follow system settings
2. **`dark` class on `<html>`**: Programmatic control

We chose approach 2 because we wanted to support manual theme switching, not be locked to system settings.

This choice dug a pit when writing E2E tests later: axe-core's accessibility tests run in light mode by default. To test dark mode, you must manually:

```typescript
await page.evaluate(() => document.documentElement.classList.add('dark'))
await page.waitForTimeout(200) // CSS transitions settle
```

The first version of a11y tests completely missed dark mode—only light mode passed, dark mode never ran. The reviewer caught this in the first review round.

---

## WCAG Contrast: The rgba Trap

This was the most interesting pit in the entire visual refactor.

The initial label-3 used `rgba(60,60,67,0.36)`. On white backgrounds this looks like light gray—visually OK, feels "Apple-ish."

Then axe-core reported a color-contrast violation: contrast ratio only 1.7:1, far below WCAG AA's required 4.5:1.

My first reaction was to increase opacity: change to `rgba(60,60,67,0.72)`.

Not enough. axe still complained.

Then I realized: **axe-core's contrast algorithm doesn't always correctly penetrate transparency stacks when processing RGBA colors**. When an `rgba` text color sits on a container that's also semi-transparent, axe stops tracing at some layer, gets the wrong background color, and calculates wrong contrast ratios.

The fix: switch to solid hex. `rgba(60,60,67,0.36)` → `#6e6e75`.

These two colors look nearly identical on white backgrounds, but axe correctly calculates `#6e6e75`'s contrast ratio as 4.5:1 (barely meeting WCAG AA), because it's solid color with no transparency tracing issues.

**Principle: For text color tokens that need WCAG compliance, use solid hex, not rgba.** Save rgba for decorative elements (borders, fills, dividers).

---

## Visual Details: Subtle but Important Bits

**Hero Header gradient**: The homepage top header background uses a 165° gradient from deep blue to light blue. Light/dark each have their own set:

```css
/* Light */
--home-header-bg: linear-gradient(165deg,#1c3a8a 0%,#2d5be3 35%,#4f83f1 65%,#6ea8f7 100%);

/* Dark */
--home-header-bg: linear-gradient(165deg,#0d0d1e 0%,#1a1040 40%,#2d1b69 80%,#1a1040 100%);
```

**Spring animations**: All interactive elements use spring curves: `cubic-bezier(0.34,1.2,0.64,1)`. This curve has slight overshoot, giving hover/tap a springy feel that's more lively than standard ease-out.

**Hidden scrollbars**: `.home-feed { scrollbar-width: none; }` + `-webkit-scrollbar: display: none`. This detail is crucial—apps with visible scrollbars automatically drop a tier compared to those without.

**Icon system**: Event icons in UpcomingChips use keyword matching: "birthday→🎂, dinner→🍽, medical→🏥, flight→✈️". This isn't production-grade (ideally we'd have an SVG icon set), but for MVP phase emoji icons work, and user feedback was "this is cute."

---

## The Result

The most direct before/after comparison: previously it was a tool app, afterward it became an app family members would actually open and use.

This isn't mysticism. It's about trust—a well-designed interface makes users feel "this product was made thoughtfully," and they become more willing to trust it with managing their schedules.

The key to implementing Apple-style UI with AI isn't letting it "freestyle," but giving it a clear constraint system: tokens, spacing grids, border radius specs, layer rules. The clearer the constraints, the less likely AI will do weird things.

The /apple-style-ui skill is the carrier for this constraint set. When AI reads these rules, it has direction for execution.
